summary:
The Avar Khaganate (c. 567–822 AD) was a powerful nomadic empire that dominated the Carpathian Basin and surrounding regions for over two centuries, exerting enormous pressure on the Byzantine Empire, the Frankish kingdoms, and the Slavic peoples of Central and Eastern Europe. The Avars — a Turkic or mixed Turkic-Mongolic people who migrated westward from Central Asia — arrived in Europe around 560 AD and rapidly constructed a formidable military confederation centered in the Hungarian plain.

The Avars contributed significantly to the transformation of early medieval Europe. They introduced the stirrup to Europe — a technology that revolutionized cavalry warfare — and their repeated raids and sieges of Byzantine territories, including the famous joint Avar-Sassanid siege of Constantinople in 626 AD, stretched Byzantine resources and helped precipitate the catastrophic Byzantine-Persian wars that left both empires weakened before the Islamic conquests. The Avars also drove, absorbed, or dominated large Slavic populations, using Slavic warriors as infantry in their armies and inadvertently spreading Slavic settlement across much of the Balkans, Germany, and Eastern Europe. The Avar Khaganate was ultimately destroyed by Charlemagne's systematic military campaigns between 791 and 803 AD, which eliminated the Avar ruling class and dispersed their treasure — reportedly enormous quantities of gold accumulated over centuries of tribute-taking — to Frankish churches and nobles.
